title: Tabernaemontana peninsularis
taxonomy: Tabernaemontana peninsularis is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. It belongs to the genus Tabernaemontana, which comprises about 100 species of flowering plants native to tropical and subtropical regions of the Old World. The plant is further classified into the order Gentianales and the clade Asterids. Tabernaemontana peninsularis is native to the Malay Peninsula, where it grows as a shrub or small tree in tropical forests. As a member of the Apocynaceae family, it shares characteristics with other plants such as milkweeds and oleander.
uses: Tabernaemontana peninsularis is a species of plant native to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, and its leaves, bark, and roots are traditionally used in various medicinal applications. The plant is said to possess antidiabetic, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties, making it a potential treatment for various health conditions. In traditional medicine, the plant is used to treat fever, skin infections, and respiratory issues. Additionally, extracts from the plant have been studied for their potential to inhibit the growth of certain types of cancer cells. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Tabernaemontana peninsularis for medicinal purposes.

intro: Tabernaemontana peninsularis, also known as the Peninsular yellowwood, is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including its ability to treat fever, cough, and respiratory infections, as well as its potential to lower blood pressure and prevent cancer due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Tabernaemontana peninsularis include alkaloids such as voacangine and voacristine, which have been shown to possess anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory activities. In traditional herbal medicine, Tabernaemontana peninsularis is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used to make herbal teas, tinctures, and capsules.
